---
title: "Decipher Visualizations Tutorial"
subtitle: "<i>How to visualize Decipher outputs</i>"
author: "Edgar Basto"
title-block-banner: Background.png
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: scroll
    code-tools: false
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    embed-resources: true
    highlight-style: github
editor: visual
date: today
theme: 
  light: [flatly, insigene-light.scss]
  dark: [darkly, insigene-dark.scss]
bibliography: bibliography.bib
lightbox: auto
execute:
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
---


# 0. environment
To run this analysis we use the following docker images

* `ebasto/decipherc2c@sha256:9ec8bbb7eca692856e9fd7c50346c78eac6ae24da8bd2e87b1f36998465bd1b0`

These images contain all the libraries Decipher and CellOracle are dependent on. Please pull them from dockerhub. We don't use CellOracle's default dockerhub image as it contains a bug that makes the analysis not reproducible.

In addition to the docker images, currently the `Decipher` package is available as a development package from `https://github.com/INSiGENe/Decipher`, specifically branch `v2`. Please clone this branch from the repository, and execute the docker containers within it as such:

``` 
#| eval: false
#| include: true
#for Decipher
docker run --rm -it -v "$(pwd):/home/project" -w /home/project ebasto/decipherc2c@sha256:9ec8bbb7eca692856e9fd7c50346c78eac6ae24da8bd2e87b1f36998465bd1b0 bash
```

This generates an interactive bash session, within it you can type `R` to start an R session for R-based steps or, in the case of CellOracle, execute a python script.

```{r load needed packages, libraries and set a seed}
#| eval: true
#| include: true
# Running this in the root of the Decipher repository
renv::restore()   # ensure reproducible library state, say yes if asked to install packages (will install from cache)
library(devtools)
load_all()
library(data.table)
set.seed(123)
```

Overview

This vignette shows how to load Decipher results and generate common visualizations: 

* volcano plots
* the prioritized Decipher map
* LR→TF heatmaps 
* sorted TF heatmaps per cluster 
* pubmed target-gene heatmaps, and 
* network plots.

0. Setup / paths
These are the main paths needed for the visualizations, dataset_path is the path where Decipher was previously run. Most figures (with the exception of the Decipher plot will be saved in the figures folder indicated below)

```{r set up the paths}
#| eval: true
#only change these 
dataset_path <- "results/sample_analysis"
figures_folder <- "figures"

#automatically populated from the two paths above
pre_processing_filepath <- file.path(dataset_path, "pre_processing")
reference_filepath <- file.path("reference_data")
output_data_filepath <- file.path(dataset_path, "data")
output_figures_filepath <- file.path(dataset_path, "figures")
dir.create(figures_folder, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
```

1. Load Data & Pre-process for plotting
First we load all data that was produced by the pipeline. This data is pre-processed to be compatible with the plotting we did for the manuscript

```{r load data and pre-process for plotting}
#| eval: true
# Load saved Decipher results and run pre-processing helper
decipher_results <- safe_load(file.path(output_data_filepath, "decipher_scores_by_cluster.rds"))
decipher_pre_processed <- preProcessDecipher(decipher_results)

results_preprocessed <- list()
results_preprocessed <- add_if_not_null(results_preprocessed, "Decipher", decipher_pre_processed)

# Prepare objects used for comparison/correlation plots
results_for_correlation <- lapply(names(results_preprocessed), function(name) {
  prepareForCorrelation(name, results_preprocessed[[name]])
})
names(results_for_correlation) <- names(results_preprocessed)

decipher_results_for_comparison <- lapply(decipher_results, "renameDecipherScore")
results_for_comparison <- list()
results_for_comparison <- add_if_not_null(results_for_comparison, "Decipher", decipher_results_for_comparison)
```

2. Volcano plot of TF activity by cell-type
Our first plot is very simple, it's the change in TF activity by cell-type. Results are saved to the figures_folder by default.

```{r volcano plots of TF activty by cell-type}
#| eval: true
selected_cluster <- names(decipher_results)[1]
differentialTFActivityVolcanoPlotByCluster(
  output_data_filepath,
  selected_cluster,
  figures_folder,
  p_threshold = 0.01,
  fc_threhsold = 2.883,
  output_filename = "diff_regulong_act_volcano_plot"
)
# Output saved under figures_folder
```

3. Decipher prioritized map (heatmap of top interactions). 
Results are saved in the dataset_path (figures folder)

```{r Decipher prioritized map}
#| eval: true
plotDecipherPrioritizedMap(
  dataset_path,
  top_n = 6,
  priority_receiver_cells = selected_cluster,
  dataset_name = "sample_dataset",
  width = 21,
  height = 11
)
# Note: this function may display in-session; save/export as needed.
```

4. LR → TF heatmap (TFs tied to upstream LR pairs). 
Results are saved to the figures_folder by default.

```{r LR to TF heatmap}
#| eval: true
plotLRTFHeatmap(
  output_data_filepath,
  selected_cluster,
  figures_folder,
  p_threshold = 0.01,
  output_name = "lr_tf_heatmap",
  n_interactions = 10,
  min_abs_decipher_score = 0.4,
  min_delta_pagoda = 2
)
```

5. Sorted TF heatmaps (per cluster)
This plot plots a heatmap of deltas in TF activity by cluster. Each heatmap is sorted by the selected_cluster parameter, thus allowing us to see whether the stronger patterns we may see in some cell-types align with the TF activity in other cell types. Results are saved to the figures_folder by default.

```{r sorted TF heatmap}
#| eval: true
selected_clusters <- names(decipher_results)
# load regulon deltas
file_path <- file.path(output_data_filepath, "regulon_deltas_by_cluster.rds")
regulon_deltas_by_cluster <- load_regulon_data(file_path, selected_clusters)

# Calculate a global absolute max for consistent color scales
absolute_max <- find_absolute_max_simple(regulon_deltas_by_cluster)
if (is.na(absolute_max) || absolute_max <= 0) {
  warning("Could not determine a valid absolute max deltaPagoda. Setting scale limit to 1.")
  absolute_max <- 1
}

top_n <- 15
for (cell_type in selected_clusters) {
  p <- plotClusterSortedTFHeatmap(
    selected_cluster = cell_type,
    top_n = top_n,
    deltas_by_cluster = regulon_deltas_by_cluster,
    global_max = absolute_max   # optional: fix color scale across multiple plots
  )

  file_name <- paste0("sorted_tf_deltas_", cell_type, "_", top_n, ".png")
  ggsave(file.path(figures_folder, file_name), p, width = 8, height = 3)
  file_name_csv <- paste0("sorted_tf_deltas_", cell_type, "_", top_n, ".csv")
  write.csv(p$data, file.path(figures_folder, file_name_csv), row.names = TRUE)
}

print(p)

```


6. PubMed target-gene heatmaps
This plot is a way for us to understand the pruned GRNs we use to calculte TF activity. Because these GRNs may potentially cover many genes, we identify the top n_pubmed number of genes (by number of publications, as a proxy of a gene being understood). The output heatmap is a binary indicator of whether a gene does belong to a particular GRN. Results are saved to the figures_folder by default.

```{r pubmed target genes visualizations}
#| eval: true
n_pubmed <- 40
plot_pubmed_tg_heatmaps(
  output_data_filepath,
  selected_clusters[1],
  n_pubmed
)
```

7. Network plots (sender / receiver network visualisations)
Lastly this function plots four layers of Decipher (sender CT, ligand, receptor, TF). Each plot is specific to a single receiver cell type, hence why we loop over the "target_cluster". Results are saved to the figures_folder by default.

```{r network plots}
#| eval: true
selected_clusters <- names(decipher_results)
# Example: visualize networks for a target receiver cluster (or vector of clusters)
target_clusters <- selected_clusters[3]
sender_cts <- selected_clusters

for (cl in target_clusters) {
  generate_network_plot(
    "sample_analysis",
    cl,
    output_data_filepath,
    sender_cts,
    figures_folder,
    top_interactions = NULL,
    scaling_global_sender_ligand_max = 1/1.2,
    scaling_global_decipher_score_max = 1/1.5,
    n_top_regulons = 10
  )
}

```

And here is what that plot looks like
```{r}
#| label: show_png_knitr
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "My PNG (knitr::include_graphics)"
knitr::include_graphics("figures/sample_analysis_B_cells_network_map.png")
```

Session Info

<details>

<summary>Show Details</summary>

```{r}
#| eval: true
sessionInfo()
````

</details>
